text.fullText:
The proposed rule on rate transparency constitutes a serious breach of privacy by FMCSA. Requiring disclosure of private business information undermines the trust and
confidentiality that form the foundation of broker-client relationships.

This proposed rule represents an overreach of FMCSA's regulatory authority. The agency's role is to oversee safety and compliance, not to dictate private business arrangements or interfere in lawful contracts.

The proposed regulation raises significant concerns under the Commerce Clause of the
U.S. Constitution. Imposing these mandates interferes with free trade and imposes undue burdens on brokers, carriers, and shippers.

Mandating rate transparency increases the risk of fraud by exposing sensitive business
information to bad actors. Brokers serve as a critical layer of protection, and this rule jeopardizes their ability to safeguard the industry.

This regulation could enable fraudulent carriers to gain access to critical business
information, deepening the fraud pandemic already harming the industry.

This rule threatens to destabilize an already fragile freight market still recovering from the e0ects of the COVID-19 pandemic and extreme inflation. The economic consequences could harm the broader American economy.

The expansion of 371.3 disregards the evolution of the transportation industry. Its original intent in 1980 was to prevent freight rebating in a very di0erent marketplace. This rule fails to align with the current operational realities of brokers.

This proposed rule adds unnecessary restrictions that impede the free market. Even the
Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) once stated its goal was to remove such barriers
to ensure a thriving marketplace.

The rule may violate the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 by compelling the disclosure of sensitive pricing and business information, which could severely damage brokers’
competitive standing.

The Supreme Court's recent decision overturning the Chevron deference raises legal
concerns about this rulemaking. FMCSA risks overstepping its legal authority by imposing such intrusive regulations.

The proposed rule undermines capitalism. Shippers and carriers have the freedom to
choose brokers or other business partners based on mutually agreed terms. Imposing
mandatory transparency disrupts this freedom.

The FMCSA attempt to require market participants to reveal private business information is a broad overreach and a misinterpretation of the breadth of FMCSA’s authority.
Some of the statutes that FMCSA cites as the basis for its authority likely do not authorize required disclosure to private third parties. And while there may be a broad grant of authority, the ICC and FMCSA have not regulated broker charges and have repeatedly recognized that brokers benefit both shippers and carriers. Additionally, Congress deregulated the trucking industry with an intent to rely on market forces, which makes it peculiar for FMCSA to now find it has authority to interfere with a broker’s commercial relations.

This lack of information about another party’s profit margin is not unique to brokerage, indeed, it is not unique to transportation. Just as a shipper has no idea what a carrier’s margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. There is no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker differently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker differently than a motor carrier that might contract with a shipper directly.

Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent
expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law
counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of 410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect trade secrets.
DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information, including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18 U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed clearly fall within the definition of a “trade secret.”
